title: STANDARD FOR CHOCOLATE AND CHOCOLATE PRODUCTS CXS 87-1981

sections.section: STANDARD FOR CHOCOLATE AND CHOCOLATE PRODUCTS CXS 87-1981
sections.summary: Gives the title of the Codex Standard for Chocolate and Chocolate Products (CXS 87‑1981) and records its adoption in 1981, revision in 2003 and amendments in 2016 and 2022.

Records a 2022 amendment replacing previous Section 5.3 on non‑retail container labelling with a requirement to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non‑Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346‑2021).

States the standard applies to chocolate and chocolate products listed in Section 2 intended for human consumption and specifies they shall be prepared from cocoa and cocoa materials with sugars and may contain sweeteners, milk products, flavourings and other food ingredients.

Defines 'chocolate' as a homogeneous product from cocoa materials possibly combined with milk, sugars, sweeteners and permitted additives, limits other added edible foodstuffs to 40% of finished product and vegetable fats other than cocoa butter to 5% (without reducing minimum cocoa material requirements), and introduces detailed compositional specifications in Section 2.1.

Lists compositional requirements for various chocolate types (examples include: Chocolate—on a dry matter basis ≥35% total cocoa solids, ≥18% cocoa butter, ≥14% fat‑free cocoa solids; Sweet Chocolate ≥30% total cocoa solids with ≥18% cocoa butter and ≥12% fat‑free cocoa solids; Couverture ≥35% total with ≥31% cocoa butter; Milk Chocolate and other types have specified minimum cocoa and milk solids), and defines other products such as white chocolate, gianduja and chocolate para mesa with their minimum contents.

Defines 'Chocolate' (also called bittersweet/semi‑sweet/dark) as containing on a dry matter basis not less than 35% total cocoa solids (of which at least 18% cocoa butter and 14% fat‑free cocoa solids) and defines 'Chocolate a la taza' as that product with a maximum of 8% m/m flour and/or starch from wheat, maize or rice.

Sweet Chocolate must contain on a dry matter basis at least 30% total cocoa solids, of which at least 18% shall be cocoa butter and at least 12% fat-free cocoa solids; the related product "chocolate familiar a la taza" is the same type but may contain up to 18% (m/m) wheat, maize or rice flour/starch.
Couverture Chocolate must contain on a dry matter basis not less than 35% total cocoa solids, including at least 31% cocoa butter and at least 2.5% fat-free cocoa solids.

Milk Chocolate must contain on a dry matter basis not less than 25% cocoa solids (including at least 2.5% fat-free cocoa solids) and a specified minimum of milk solids between 12% and 14% (including milk fat 2.5–3.5%); the authority having jurisdiction applies the milk solids/milk fat minima, defines "milk solids" as milk ingredients in natural proportions (with milk fat optionally added or removed), and may set a minimum for cocoa butter plus milk fat where required.

Family Milk Chocolate must contain on a dry matter basis at least 20% cocoa solids (including at least 2.5% fat-free cocoa solids) and at least 20% milk solids (including at least 5% milk fat); "milk solids" means milk ingredients in natural proportions except milk fat may be added or removed, and the competent authority may set a minimum for cocoa butter plus milk fat.

Milk Chocolate Couverture must contain on a dry matter basis not less than 25% cocoa solids (including at least 2.5% non-fat cocoa solids), not less than 14% milk solids (including at least 3.5% milk fat) and a total fat of not less than 31%, with "milk solids" defined as milk ingredients in their natural proportions except milk fat may be added or removed.
This is a section heading for 'Other chocolate products' and contains no substantive provisions by itself.

White Chocolate must contain on a dry matter basis at least 20% cocoa butter and at least 14% milk solids (with minimum milk fat in the 2.5–3.5% range as applied by the authority); "milk solids" is defined as milk ingredients in natural proportions (milk fat may be added or removed), and the competent authority may set a minimum for cocoa butter plus milk fat.

Gianduja Chocolate is made from chocolate with at least 32% total dry cocoa solids (including at least 8% dry non‑fat cocoa solids) combined with finely ground hazelnuts such that hazelnut content is between 20% and 40%; permitted additions include (a) milk/dry milk by evaporation provided finished product contains no more than 5% dry milk solids, and (b) whole or broken almonds, hazelnuts and other nuts provided that together with the ground hazelnuts they do not exceed 60% of total weight.

Gianduja Milk Chocolate is obtained from milk chocolate with a minimum dry milk solids content of 10% combined with finely ground hazelnuts to give 15–40% hazelnut content; "milk solids" are milk ingredients in natural proportions (milk fat may be added/removed), almonds/hazelnuts/other nuts may be added provided they and the ground hazelnuts do not exceed 60% of total weight, and the competent authority may set a minimum for cocoa butter plus milk fat.
This section defines 'Chocolate para mesa' as unrefined chocolate in which sugar grain size exceeds 70 microns.
Chocolate para mesa must contain on a dry matter basis at least 20% total cocoa solids, including at least 11% cocoa butter and at least 9% fat-free cocoa solids.
Semi-bitter Chocolate para mesa must contain on a dry matter basis not less than 30% total cocoa solids, including at least 15% cocoa butter and at least 14% fat-free cocoa solids.
Bitter Chocolate para mesa must contain on a dry matter basis at least 40% total cocoa solids, including at least 22% cocoa butter and at least 18% fat-free cocoa solids.

Section 2.2 covers chocolate types/forms (vermicelli, flakes, filled chocolate, single‑bite chocolates/pralines) including production descriptions and compositional minima; it defines vermicelli/flakes by processing and shape and sets compositional minima for those and for filled chocolates (see subsections) and includes a summary table of the compositional requirements.

Chocolate Vermicelli and Chocolate Flakes are cocoa products produced by mixing, extrusion and hardening to give crisp texture, presented as short cylindrical grains (vermicelli) or small flat pieces (flakes).
Chocolate Vermicelli/Chocolate Flakes must contain on a dry matter basis at least 32% total cocoa solids, of which at least 12% shall be cocoa butter and 14% fat-free cocoa solids.

Milk Chocolate Vermicelli/Milk Chocolate Flakes must contain on a dry matter basis at least 20% cocoa solids (including at least 2.5% fat-free cocoa solids) and at least 12% milk solids (including at least 3% milk fat); "milk solids" are milk ingredients in natural proportions (milk fat may be added/removed) and the competent authority may set a minimum for cocoa butter plus milk fat.

Filled Chocolate is a product coated with one or more of the chocolates defined in Section 2.1 (excluding chocolate a la taza, chocolate familiar a la taza and chocolate para mesa) whose center is compositionally distinct from the coating; the coating must constitute at least 25% of total product weight, the product excludes flour confectionery/pastry/biscuit/ice cream, and any center components covered by a separate Codex standard must comply with that standard.

A Chocolate or Praline is a single mouthful product in which the chocolate component is at least 25% of total weight and which consists of filled chocolate or one or more of the chocolates defined under Section 2.1 (excluding chocolate a la taza, chocolate familiar a la taza and chocolate para mesa).

sections.section: TABLE 1. SUMMARY TABLE OF COMPOSITIONAL REQUIREMENTS OF SECTION 2 1
sections.summary: Table 1 is a summary table of compositional requirements (percentages on a dry matter basis after deducting authorized additional edible foodstuffs) for the chocolate types in Section 2.1, listing minima for cocoa butter, fat‑free cocoa solids, total cocoa solids, milk fat, total milk solids, starch/flour and hazelnuts where applicable.

Table header listing composition attributes used for chocolate types: cocoa butter, fat‑free cocoa solids, total cocoa solids, milk fat, total milk solids, starch/flour, and hazelnuts (non‑substantive on its own).
Section heading for 'CHOCOLATE TYPES (COMPOSITION)' with no substantive compositional requirements beyond the heading itself.
Defines 'Chocolate' composition requiring at least 18% cocoa butter, at least 14% fat‑free cocoa solids and at least 35% total cocoa solids.
'Chocolate a la taza' composition: minimum 18% cocoa butter, minimum 14% fat‑free cocoa solids, minimum 35% total cocoa solids, and starch/flour less than 8%.
'Sweet Chocolat' composition requiring at least 18% cocoa butter, at least 12% fat‑free cocoa solids and at least 30% total cocoa solids.
'Chocolate familiar a la taza' composition: minimum 18% cocoa butter, minimum 12% fat‑free cocoa solids, minimum 30% total cocoa solids, and starch/flour less than 18%.
'Couverture Chocolate' composition requiring at least 31% cocoa butter, at least 2.5% fat‑free cocoa solids and at least 35% total cocoa solids.
'Milk Chocolate' composition requiring at least 2.5% fat‑free cocoa solids and at least 25% total cocoa solids, with milk fat in the range 2.5–3.5% and total milk solids 12–14%.
'Family Milk Chocolate' composition requiring at least 2.5% fat‑free cocoa solids, at least 20% total cocoa solids, milk fat at least 5% and total milk solids at least 20%.
'Milk Chocolate couverture' composition requiring at least 2.5% fat‑free cocoa solids, at least 25% total cocoa solids, milk fat at least 3.5% and total milk solids at least 14%.
Section heading 'Other chocolate products' with no substantive compositional details in the text provided.
'White Chocolate' composition requiring at least 20% cocoa butter, milk fat in the range 2.5–3.5% and total milk solids at least 14%.
'Gianduja Chocolate' composition requiring at least 8% fat‑free cocoa solids, at least 32% total cocoa solids and hazelnut content between 20% and 40%.

'Gianduja Milk Chocolate' composition requiring at least 2.5% fat‑free cocoa solids, at least 25% total cocoa solids, milk fat 2.5–3.5%, total milk solids at least 10%, and hazelnut content between 15% and 40%.
Section heading 'Chocolate para mesa' with no substantive compositional details in the text provided.
Section heading 'CHOCOLATE TYPES (forms)' only, without substantive requirements in the provided text.
'Chocolate Vermicelli / Chocolate Flakes' composition requiring minimums of approximately 12% cocoa butter, 14% fat‑free cocoa solids and 32% total cocoa solids (table row of compositional minima).
Classification heading 'Filled Chocolate (see section 2.2.2)' only, not substantive on its own.
Classification heading 'A Chocolate or Praline (see section 2.2.3)' only, not substantive on its own.

Permits certain additives for chocolate products: acidity regulators, antioxidants, bulking agents, surface‑decoration colours, emulsifiers, glazing agents and sweeteners per Tables 1–2 of the GSFA (CXS 192‑1995) with only specified Table 3 additives allowed; flavourings must follow CXG 66‑2008 and not imitate chocolate or milk (vanillin and ethyl vanillin are exceptions up to 1000 mg/kg combined), and processing aids must follow CXG 75‑2010 with hexane limited to 1 mg/kg on a fat‑content basis.

sections.section: 3.1 Acidity regulators, antioxidants, bulking agents, colours (for surface decoration purposes only), emulsifiers, glazing agents and sweeteners used in accordance with Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) in food category 05.1.4 (Chocolate and chocolate products) and its parent food categories are acceptable for use in foods conforming to this standard. Only certain Table 3 food additives (as indicated in Table 3) are acceptable for use in foods conforming to this standard.
sections.summary: Permits use of acidity regulators, antioxidants, bulking agents, surface-decoration colours, emulsifiers, glazing agents and sweeteners in chocolate (food category 05.1.4 and parents) only as listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995), and allows only certain Table 3 additives where indicated in Table 3.

sections.section: 3.2 The flavourings used in products covered by this standard should comply with the Guidelines for the Use of Flavourings (CXG 66-2008). Only those flavourings that do not imitate chocolate or milk flavours are permitted at GMP for products described under 2.1 and 2.2, except for vanillin and ethyl vanillin at a maximum level of 1000 mg/kg, singly or in combination.
sections.summary: Requires flavourings to comply with the Guidelines for the Use of Flavourings (CXG 66-2008) and permits only flavourings that do not imitate chocolate or milk at GMP for products under Sections 2.1 and 2.2, except vanillin and ethyl vanillin which may be used up to 1000 mg/kg singly or combined.
Specifies processing aids consistent with CXG 75-2010 and sets a maximum hexane residue limit of 1 mg/kg (calculated on a fat content basis) for hexane (62–82°C) used as a processing aid.

Recommends preparing and handling products in accordance with Codex hygienic texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1-1969, Code for Low Moisture Foods CXC 75-2015 and other relevant Codes of Practice) and requires compliance with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.

Adds specific labelling requirements beyond the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre-packaged Foods (CXS 1/119‑85), including product naming rules (see Section 5.1 subsections), declarations when sugars are replaced by sweeteners or vegetable fats are used, minimum cocoa content declarations where required, and non-retail container labelling per CXS 346-2021.

Requires products defined under Section 2.2.2 to be designated 'Filled Chocolate', 'X Filled Chocolate', 'Chocolate with X Filling' or 'Chocolate with X Centre' (X descriptive of filling), allows specifying the external coating type per Section 5.1.1 designations, and mandates a clear statement informing consumers of the nature of the centre.
States that single mouthful products under Section 2.2.3 shall be designated 'A Chocolate' or 'Praline'.

Allows assortments of products (excluding certain table chocolates) to be labelled with an assortment designation (e.g. 'Assorted Chocolates') provided there is either a single ingredients list for the assortment or separate ingredient lists by product.

Requires any characterizing flavour other than chocolate, and particularly aromatic ingredients that characterize the product, to be included in the product name or designation (e.g. 'Mocca Chocolate').

Permits use of the term 'chocolate' for products not defined by this standard whose chocolate taste derives solely from non‑fat cocoa solids, subject to national provisions or customary practice and provided they cannot be confused with products defined in this standard.

When required by the competent authority, products in Section 2.1 (except white chocolate) must carry a declaration of cocoa solids expressed as a percentage calculated on the chocolate part after deducting other permitted edible foodstuffs.
States that labelling of non-retail containers should follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).

Prescribes methods of analysis and sampling for chocolate products, listing approved AOAC/IOCCC methods for determinations (centre/coating, cocoa butter, fat‑free cocoa and milk solids, milk fat, moisture, total fat) and providing specific methods and interpretation guidance for detection and quantification of non‑cocoa butter vegetable fats (AOCS, GC‑FID references) with a requirement to provide documentation on commercial blends to competent authorities.

sections.section: 6.1 Determination of centre and coating of filled chocolate
sections.summary: Specifies that determination of the centre and coating of filled chocolate shall use any methods approved for the specific chocolate coating type and for the type of centre concerned.
States that cocoa butter content shall be determined according to AOAC 963.15 or IOCCC 14-1972.
Specifies determination of fat‑free cocoa solids according to AOAC 931.05.
Specifies determination of fat‑free milk solids according to IOCCC 17-1973 or AOAC 939.02.
Specifies determination of milk fat according to IOCCC 5-1962 or AOAC methods 945.34, 925.41B, or 920.80.
Specifies determination of moisture by IOCCC 26-1988 or AOAC 977.10 (Karl Fischer method), or by AOAC 931.04 or IOCCC 1-1952 (gravimetric methods).
Specifies determination of total fat according to AOAC 963.15.

sections.section: 6.8 Determination of non-cocoa butter vegetable fat in chocolate and chocolate products
sections.summary: Sets out the best-available methods (subject to further improvement) and documentation requirements for detecting and quantifying non‑cocoa‑butter vegetable fats in chocolate: detection by sterol breakdown (AOCS Ce 10/02), quantitative triacylglycerol profiling (GC‑FID for C50, C52, C54) with milk‑fat correction for milk chocolate, interpretation/calculation methods depending on whether the vegetable‑fat type is known (J. Amer. Oil Chem. Soc. 1980 or 1982), and notes that the quantitative method targets cocoa‑butter equivalents (SOS triglycerides) while other vegetable fats are assessed by conventional fatty acid/triacylglycerol analyses; competent authorities may request documentation on commercial fat blends.

sections.section: 6.8.1 Detection of Non-Cocoa Butter Vegetable Fats in Chocolate
sections.summary: Specifies detection of sterol breakdown products from refined vegetable fats added to chocolate using method AOCS Ce 10/02 (02).

sections.section: 6.8.2 Quantitative Determination of Non-Cocoa Butter Vegetable Fats 2
sections.summary: Describes quantitative determination of non‑cocoa butter vegetable fats by GC‑FID analysis of triacylglycerols (C50, C52, C54) per J. Amer. Oil Chem. Soc. (1980), noting that milk chocolate requires correction for milk fat.
